Note: [British North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/British_North-West_Frontier_Province "British North-West Frontier Province") era district borders are not an exact match in the present-day due to various bifurcations to district borders — which since created new districts — throughout the region during the post-independence era that have taken into account population increases.

infobox.value: ([Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): پېښور ولسوالۍ, [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko_language "Hindko language"): ضلع پشور‎, [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): ضلع پشاور‎) is a [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan") in the [Peshawar Division](/wiki/Peshawar_Division "Peshawar Division") of the [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") province of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). It is located about 160 km west of the Pakistan's capital [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"). The district headquarter is the city of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), which is also the capital of [Khyber Paktunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Peshawar District** ([Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): پېښور ولسوالۍ, [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko_language "Hindko language"): ضلع پشور‎, [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): ضلع پشاور‎) is a [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan") in the [Peshawar Division](/wiki/Peshawar_Division "Peshawar Division") of the [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") province of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). It is located about 160 km west of the Pakistan's capital [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"). The district headquarter is the city of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), which is also the capital of [Khyber Paktunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=). *(March 2026)* |

This district and the city has seen the rise and fall of many civilizations. It was once the center of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and has subsequently been ruled by [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Kushans](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), [Afghans](/wiki/Afghans "Afghans"), [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") and the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Peshawar district was annexed by the British from its former [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") rulers after the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_war "Second Anglo-Sikh war") of 1848–1849.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The original district of Peshawar was a district of the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)") of [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

After the independence of Pakistan in 1947, the old Peshawar District became [Peshawar Division](/wiki/Peshawar_Division "Peshawar Division") comprising the current districts of Peshawar, Charsadda and Nowshera. In July 1988, the former Charsadda tehsil was separated and attained the status of a separate district. In 1990, the Nowshera tehsil also was upgraded to the status of a district.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDistrict_census_report_of_Nowshera19981-6) Under the latest revision of Pakistan's administrative structure, promulgated in 2001, Peshawar was also given the status of a city district.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Population")]

As of the [2023 census](/wiki/2023_Pakistani_census "2023 Pakistani census"), Peshawar district has 690,976 households and a population of 4,758,762. The district has a sex ratio of 103.99 males to 100 females and a literacy rate of 53%: 65% for males and 41% for females. 1,355,625 (28.54% of the surveyed population) are under 10 years of age. Approximately 1,905,975 (40.05%) live in urban areas.[[1]](#cite_note-2023_census-1)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Languages")]

Languages of Peshawar district (2023)

1. [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") (93.1%)
2. [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") (3.79%)
3. [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (1.80%)
4. [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (0.55%)
5. Others (0.79%)

At the time of the 2023, 93.07% of the district’s population spoke [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto"), 3.79% [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko"), 1.8% [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and 0.55% [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") as their first language.[[8]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-8) Due to the settlement of Afghan refugees and language shift Pashto has gained ground at the expense of Hindko.

According to linguists, the nearly-extinct [Tirahi language](/wiki/Tirahi_language "Tirahi language") was likely spoken in the region before the arrival of the Afghans.[[9]](#cite_note-morg-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

The main ethnic groups in Peshawar district are:

* [Hindkowan](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans")
* [Gujjar](/wiki/Gurjar "Gurjar")[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtun "Pashtun")
* [Kohistani](/wiki/Indus_Kohistani_people "Indus Kohistani people")

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religions in Peshawar district (2023)[[13]](#cite_note-2023censusreligion-13)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Pakistan "Christianity in Pakistan") | |  | 0.70% | |

[Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") make up the majority in Peshawar. Peshawar once had significant minorities of [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") living in the small towns and [Peshawar city](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), but most fled during Partition. 1,800 Hindus and 1,400 Sikhs remain in the district. [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") however have continued to grow.

### 1897 Gazetteer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1897 Gazetteer")]

According to the 1897 district gazetteer, published during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), most people living in Peshawar valley were [Pathans](/wiki/Pathans "Pathans") and belonged to an agricultural community but there was also large number of [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people") and [Hindkowan](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans") communities living in the valley; all three ethnolinguistic groups religiously belonged to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism").[[23]](#cite_note-Gazetteer1897-23) Most people in the [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") city spoke and understood [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), while economic elites from small towns such as big feudal [Khan](/wiki/Khan_(surname) "Khan (surname)") families, traders, alongside many [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") also spoke [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").[[23]](#cite_note-Gazetteer1897-23) [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") was also spoken by elites of the [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") city and by traders from [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), despite a majority of the district population especially the agriculturists and Pathans only spoke [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language").[[23]](#cite_note-Gazetteer1897-23)

At the time, during the late 19th century, approximately 92–93 percent of the total population of Peshawar Valley followed Islam, while the remaining 7–8 percent followed [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and other religions.[[23]](#cite_note-Gazetteer1897-23) Persons adhering to minority religions primarily lived in major urban centres, cities, towns, and cantonments such as [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Charsadda](/wiki/Charsadda "Charsadda") (now in the [Charsadda District](/wiki/Charsadda_District,_Pakistan "Charsadda District, Pakistan")) and [Hoti](/wiki/Hoti,_Pakistan "Hoti, Pakistan") (now in the [Mardan District](/wiki/Mardan_District "Mardan District")).[[23]](#cite_note-Gazetteer1897-23) 97 percent of the population living in rural towns practiced Islam.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Refugees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Refugees")]

In recent years, Peshawar district hosts up to 20% of all [Afghan refugees in Pakistan](/wiki/Afghans_in_Pakistan "Afghans in Pakistan").[[25]](#cite_note-UNHCR-25) In 2005, Peshawar district was home to 611,501 Afghan refugees who constituted 19.7% of the district's total population.[[25]](#cite_note-UNHCR-25) Peshawar's immediate environs were home to large Afghan refugee camps, with [Jalozai](/wiki/Jalozai "Jalozai") camp hosting up to 300,000 refugees in 2001[[26]](#cite_note-Aljazeera-26) making it the largest refugee camp in Asia at the time.[[26]](#cite_note-Aljazeera-26) By 1980, 100,000 refugees a month were entering the province,[[27]](#cite_note-UNHCR2-27) with 25% of all refugees living in Peshawar district in 1981.[[27]](#cite_note-UNHCR2-27)

Afghan refugees began to be frequently accused of involvement with terrorist attacks that occurred during Pakistan's [war against radical Islamists](/wiki/War_in_North-West_Pakistan "War in North-West Pakistan").[[28]](#cite_note-PRI-28) By 2015 the Pakistani government adopted a policy to repatriate Afghan refugees, including many who had spent their entire life in Pakistan.[[28]](#cite_note-PRI-28) The policy of repatriation was also encouraged by the government of Afghanistan,[[29]](#cite_note-Constable-29) though many refugees had not registered themselves in Pakistan. Un registered refugees returning to Afghanistan without their old Afghan identification documents now have no official status in Afghanistan either.[[29]](#cite_note-Constable-29)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Administration")]

The district Peshawar is administrated by Deputy commissioner who is chief administrative, land revenue officer and the representative of government in district. DC is assisted by Additional Deputy commissioner and Assistant commissioner. Peshawar is divided into seven tehsil which are further divided into village council (rural areas) and neighbourhood council (urban areas). There are total 357 councils in peshawar district out of which 130 are neighbourhood councils and 227 are village councils.
Each tehsil has its own government known as tehsil municipal administration having an elected tehsil council and tehsil chairman except Peshawar city tehsil, which is administrated by a city local government, which is composed of an elected city council and an elected mayor.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/University_of_Peshawar.jpg/250px-University_of_Peshawar.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Peshawar.jpg)

University of Peshawar (Public Sector)

Peshawar District has many universities for various disciplines starting from [Humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [General Sciences](/wiki/General_science "General science"), [Sciences](/wiki/Science "Science"), [Engineering Sciences](/wiki/Engineering_Science "Engineering Science") to [Medical Sciences](/wiki/Medical_sciences "Medical sciences"), [Agriculture Sciences](/wiki/Agricultural_science "Agricultural science") and [Management Sciences](/wiki/Management_science "Management science"). Currently, there are nine medical colleges of which two are in the public sector, including [Khyber Medical University](/wiki/Khyber_Medical_University "Khyber Medical University"), and seven in the private sector, which all are recognised and approved by Pakistan Medical and Dental Association.[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33)

The first public sector university was the [University of Peshawar](/wiki/University_of_Peshawar "University of Peshawar")[[34]](#cite_note-34) which was established in 1950. The [University of Engineering and Technology, Peshawar](/wiki/University_of_Engineering_and_Technology,_Peshawar "University of Engineering and Technology, Peshawar")[[35]](#cite_note-35) and the [University of Agriculture, Peshawar](/wiki/Agricultural_University_Peshawar "Agricultural University Peshawar")[[36]](#cite_note-36) were also established in 1980 and 1981 respectively. The first private sector university [CECOS University of IT and Emerging Sciences](/wiki/CECOS_University_of_Information_Technology_and_Emerging_Sciences "CECOS University of Information Technology and Emerging Sciences"),[[37]](#cite_note-37) started functioning in 1986. Soon after, another university by the name of [Brains](https://www.brains.edu.pk/)[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Institute and post graduate college](https://www.brains.edu.pk/) was established. In 1995, a public sector management institute named as [Institute of Management Sciences](/wiki/Institute_of_Management_Sciences_(Peshawar) "Institute of Management Sciences (Peshawar)")[[39]](#cite_note-39) was established, which became degree awarding institute in 2005.

In 2001, four (4) new private sector universities started working in Peshawar. The name of these universities are [Qurtuba University](/wiki/Qurtuba_University "Qurtuba University"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Sarhad University of Science and IT](/wiki/Sarhad_University_of_Science_and_Information_Technology "Sarhad University of Science and Information Technology"),[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Fast University, Peshawar Campus](/wiki/National_University_of_Computer_and_Emerging_Sciences "National University of Computer and Emerging Sciences")[[42]](#cite_note-42) and [City University of Science and IT](/wiki/City_University_of_Science_and_Information_Technology,_Peshawar "City University of Science and Information Technology, Peshawar").[[43]](#cite_note-43) [Gandhara University](/wiki/Gandhara_University "Gandhara University")[[44]](#cite_note-44) was established in 2002 while [Abasyn University](/wiki/Abasyn_University "Abasyn University")[[45]](#cite_note-45) was created in 2007.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/City_University_Peshawar.jpg/250px-City_University_Peshawar.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_University_Peshawar.jpg)

City University Peshawar (Private Sector)

In 2007, [Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Government_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") established first public sector medical university, [Khyber Medical University](/wiki/Khyber_Medical_University "Khyber Medical University")[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-:4-47) and the district also has 2 public sector medical colleges, one is [Khyber Medical College](/wiki/Khyber_Medical_College "Khyber Medical College")[[47]](#cite_note-:4-47) and one for girls named as [Khyber Girls Medical College](/wiki/Khyber_Girls_Medical_College "Khyber Girls Medical College").[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33)

The first women university was established in 2012, when [Shaheed Benazir Bhutto Women University](/wiki/Shaheed_Benazir_Bhutto_Women_University "Shaheed Benazir Bhutto Women University")[[48]](#cite_note-48) started working while private sector [IQRA National University](/wiki/IQRA_National_University "IQRA National University")[[49]](#cite_note-49) was also established in 2012.

Apart from excellent range of universities, Peshawar district also has huge numbers of [further education](/wiki/Further_education "Further education") (Post School) institutes both in public and private sectors. The most renowned are [Islamia College Peshawar](/wiki/Islamia_College_Peshawar "Islamia College Peshawar"), which was established in 1913 and was upgraded to become university in 2008 and [Edwardes College Peshawar](/wiki/Edwardes_College_Peshawar "Edwardes College Peshawar"), which is the oldest of all institutions in the district started functioning in 1900.

### State of Education in Peshawar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Peshawar_District&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: State of Education in Peshawar")]

According to Pakistan District Education Rankings 2017 published by [Alif Ailaan](/wiki/Alif_Ailaan "Alif Ailaan"), Peshawar ranks 4th in Pakistan in terms of primary school infrastructure while it ranks 52nd in Pakistan for middle school infrastructure. In terms of education score, Peshawar ranks 64th in Pakistan with having a relatively low retention score. Beyond Primary Readiness in Peshawar is on the lower side as it ranks 62nd in Pakistan.

The main issues reported in [Taleem Do! App](https://web.archive.org/web/20180803074507/https://elections.alifailaan.pk/taleemdo-app/) for district Peshawar are overcrowded class rooms and lack of class rooms in schools.

Girls’ education is also one of the most reported issues in the app, with main focus on lesser number of Girls schools and also schools located at long distances.

Issues of lack of teachers, non availability of science labs and lack of High Schools were also reported.

Issues regarding the high fee collections in private schools was also reported on multiple occasions.
